meta_text: Georgian script (Mkhedruli) is unique in appearance and unrelated to other writing systems; contemporary Georgian script does not recognize capital letters, so its usage has become decorative.
source_quote: Contemporary Georgian script does not recognize capital letters and their usage has become decorative.

meta_text: The Armenian alphabet was developed around 405 AD by Mesrop Mashtots; it originally had 36 letters and is written horizontally, left to right.
source_quote: It was developed around 405 AD by Mesrop Mashtots, an Armenian linguist and ecclesiastical leader.

meta_text: Though commonly called the Thai alphabet, the Thai script is not a true alphabet but an abugida, in which full characters represent consonants with diacritical marks for vowels; Thai letters have no upper/lower case and spaces between words are not used.
source_quote: the script is not a true alphabet but an abugida, a writing system in which the full characters represent consonants with diacritical marks for vowels

meta_text: The Khmer script is an abugida used to write Khmer, the official language of Cambodia; words within the same sentence or phrase are generally run together with no spaces between them.
source_quote: Words within the same sentence or phrase are generally run together with no spaces between them.

meta_text: Lao script (Akson Lao) is the primary script used to write the Lao language and other languages in Laos, and Lao is written from left to right.
source_quote: Lao is written from left to right.

meta_text: The Sinhala script is an abugida written left to right; most of its letters are curlicues, with straight lines almost completely absent, and it has no joining characters.
source_quote: Most of the Sinhala letters are curlicues; straight lines are almost completely absent from the alphabet, and it does not have joining characters.

meta_text: The Hebrew alphabet has 22 letters, is written from right to left, and five letters have different (final) forms when used at the end of a word.
source_quote: Five letters have different forms when used at the end of a word.

meta_text: In the Korean alphabet (Hangul), letters are arranged in syllable blocks consisting of an initial consonant, a vowel, and an optional final consonant.
source_quote: They are arranged in syllable blocks consisting of an initial consonant, a vowel, and an optional final consonant.

meta_text: The Greek alphabet was derived from the earlier Phoenician alphabet and is the earliest known alphabetic script to systematically write vowels as well as consonants.
source_quote: It was derived from the earlier Phoenician alphabet, and is the earliest known alphabetic script to systematically write vowels as well as consonants.

sample_text: မြန်မာဘာသာ
meta_text: The Burmese-Mon script is distinguished from Khmer-derived scripts such as Khmer and Thai by, among other things, the use of a virāma and the round shape of letters.
source_quote: the use of a virāma, and the round shape of letters.

meta_text: Geʽez is an abugida used to write several languages of Ethiopia and Eritrea (including Amharic and Tigrinya); modern Geʽez is written from left to right.
source_quote: Modern Geʽez is written from left to right.

meta_text: The Cyrillic script is used for various languages across Eurasia; as of 2019 around 250 million people use Cyrillic as the official script for their national languages, with Russia accounting for about half of them.
source_quote: As of 2019, around 250 million people in Eurasia use Cyrillic as the official script for their national languages, with Russia accounting for about half of them.

meta_text: The Vietnamese alphabet's complex vowel system and large number of letters with diacritics, which can stack twice on the same letter, make it easy to distinguish from other writing systems that use the Latin alphabet.
source_quote: The complex vowel system and the large number of letters with diacritics, which can stack twice on the same letter

meta_text: The Polish alphabet is based on the Latin alphabet but adds letters with diacritics (the stroke/kreska ć ł ń ó ś ź, the overdot/kropka ż, and the ogonek ą ę); there are 32 letters in all.
source_quote: There are 32 letters in the Polish alphabet: 9 vowels and 23 consonants.

meta_text: The Turkish alphabet is a Latin-script alphabet of 29 letters; dotted and dotless I are distinct letters, so i becomes İ when capitalised, while I is the capital form of ı.
source_quote: Dotted and dotless I are distinct letters in Turkish such that

meta_text: Icelandic orthography uses a Latin-script alphabet of 32 letters; the letters eth (ð, capital Ð) and thorn (þ, capital Þ) are widely used in Icelandic.
source_quote: Icelandic orthography uses a Latin-script alphabet which has 32 letters.

meta_text: Thaana is the writing system of the Maldivian (Dhivehi) language spoken in the Maldives, and, like Arabic, it is written right to left.
source_quote: Thaana, like Arabic, is written right to left.

meta_text: The Tamil script is an abugida used by Tamils and Tamil speakers in India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Singapore and elsewhere; the script is syllabic, not alphabetic, and is written from left to right.
source_quote: The script is syllabic, not alphabetic, and is written from left to right.
